metadata.title: Why a Blinding Red Lamp Releases Fewer Electrons Than a Dim UV Torch
metadata.color_semantics: claude: INK #3D3929 all text; SPARK #D97757 the one accent. Content: TEAL = photon above threshold / electron ejected; CRIMSON = photon below threshold / no electron
metadata.greeting_note: hello lexicon: Spanish. Medhavy 1-word budget. Wagwan is Bear-only.
metadata.note: Refactored to Claude cut, claude-medhavy channel (claude-refactor skill). B00 = ClaudeComposerAsk cold open (Wonder register, new audio). ASK micro-beats inserted before each Manim/GRAPHIC beat (B01a, B03a, B04a, B05a, B06a, B07a, B08a). B11 = HANDOFF, B12 = ClaudeTitleOutro replacing OutroSeries/OutroCTA. Inner beats B02–B10 narration and media untouched.
metadata.audio_note: All existing inner-beat mp3s were generated with ElevenLabs NBB voice. Must regenerate ALL with kokoro af_kore for Medhavy: python3 runtime/scripts/generate_audio.py quantum-mechanics-vol1/youtube/vox-photoelectric

beats.narration_text: Let's recap with Claude. Three facts break classical theory: a sharp frequency threshold, instant emission, and electron energy set by frequency rather than intensity. Einstein's fix is that light arrives as photons, each carrying energy h times nu, and one photon strikes one electron. Kinetic energy equals h-nu minus the work function phi; below threshold that value is negative, so no electron escapes. Photons cannot pool their energy, so a blinding red lamp fails while a dim ultraviolet source ejects electrons immediately.

artifactLines: Three facts break classical theory: a sharp frequency threshold, instant emission, and electron energy set by frequency rather than intensity.
Einstein's fix is that light arrives as photons, each carrying energy h times nu, and one photon strikes one electron.
Kinetic energy equals h-nu minus the work function phi; below threshold that value is negative, so no electron escapes.
Photons cannot pool their energy, so a blinding red lamp fails while a dim ultraviolet source ejects electrons immediately.

beats.narration_text: The sodium numbers floored me — green at 2.27 eV giving zero electrons from a 10,000-watt lamp while a single 4.13 eV UV photon ejects one, because photons cannot pool their energy. I want to work the math myself. Help me: (1) convert a given wavelength to photon energy in eV so I can test it against the 2.28 eV work function; (2) compute the ejected electron's kinetic energy just above threshold; (3) show why doubling the intensity changes the electron count but never their energy.

props.segment: Why a Blinding Red Lamp Releases Fewer Electrons Than a Dim UV Torch
props.command: The sodium numbers floored me — green at 2.27 eV giving zero electrons from a 10,000-watt lamp while a single 4.13 eV UV photon ejects one, because photons cannot pool their energy. I want to work the math myself. Help me: (1) convert a given wavelength to photon energy in eV so I can test it against the 2.28 eV work function; (2) compute the ejected electron's kinetic energy just above threshold; (3) show why doubling the intensity changes the electron count but never their energy.

props.segment: Why a Blinding Red Lamp Releases Fewer Electrons Than a Dim UV Torch
props.command: claude "write a Manim scene: three experimental facts of the photoelectric effect — threshold frequency cutoff, instant emission above threshold, kinetic energy set by frequency not intensity"

beats.narration_text: Three experimental facts. One: below a threshold frequency, no electrons — ever, at any intensity. Two: above threshold, electrons emerge immediately, with no time delay. Three: the kinetic energy of each electron depends only on the frequency, not the intensity.

Classical wave theory says energy is delivered uniformly across the surface. Crank up the intensity and eventually any frequency should free an electron. So why is there a sharp threshold? Why does a dim UV source work when a blinding red lamp fails?

card.copy:
Classical waves: more intensity = more energy.
Below threshold: zero electrons at any intensity.
Why?

props.segment: Why a Blinding Red Lamp Releases Fewer Electrons Than a Dim UV Torch
props.command: claude "write a Manim scene: light as discrete photon packets each carrying h times nu, one photon strikes one electron, if energy below work function phi the electron cannot escape"

beats.narration_text: Einstein's 1905 answer: light energy is not spread continuously. It arrives as discrete packets — photons — each carrying exactly h times nu. One photon hits one electron. If that photon's energy is less than the work function phi, the electron cannot escape, period.

props.segment: Why a Blinding Red Lamp Releases Fewer Electrons Than a Dim UV Torch
props.command: claude "write a Manim scene: Einstein photoelectric equation KE = hν − φ, plot kinetic energy vs photon frequency showing the threshold cutoff and slope h"

beats.narration_text: The equation is kinetic energy equals h-nu minus phi. Above threshold: the electron escapes with kinetic energy proportional to how far above threshold you are. Below threshold: h-nu minus phi is negative — and there is no such thing as negative kinetic energy. No escape.

props.segment: Why a Blinding Red Lamp Releases Fewer Electrons Than a Dim UV Torch
props.command: claude "write a Manim scene: sodium photoelectric worked example — 546 nm green at 2.27 eV vs 300 nm UV at 4.13 eV, work function 2.28 eV, show which wavelength ejects electrons"

beats.narration_text: For sodium, the work function is 2.28 electron volts. Green light at 546 nanometers carries 2.27 electron volts per photon — one hundredth of a volt below threshold. Result: zero electrons from a ten-thousand-watt lamp. A UV photon at 300 nanometers carries 4.13 electron volts — nearly two volts above threshold. Each photon ejects one electron.

props.segment: Why a Blinding Red Lamp Releases Fewer Electrons Than a Dim UV Torch
props.command: claude "write a Manim scene: photons cannot pool energy — multiple sub-threshold red photons arrive one by one, each bounces off the electron, none can free it"

beats.narration_text: The key point: photons cannot pool their energy. A thousand red photons arriving one after another cannot combine to free one electron the way that waves could. Each photon acts alone, and a sub-threshold photon fails alone.

props.segment: Why a Blinding Red Lamp Releases Fewer Electrons Than a Dim UV Torch
props.command: claude "write a Manim scene: intensity vs frequency are independent axes in the photoelectric effect — intensity sets photon count rate, frequency sets energy per photon"

beats.narration_text: Intensity controls only how many photons arrive per second — the rate. Above threshold: more photons means more electrons, but the same kinetic energy per electron. Intensity is a count; frequency is the energy. They are independent.

props.segment: Why a Blinding Red Lamp Releases Fewer Electrons Than a Dim UV Torch
props.command: claude "write a Manim scene: Millikan's stopping potential experiment confirming Einstein's equation — plot stopping voltage vs frequency for sodium, lithium, potassium, showing linear slope equal to h"

beats.narration_text: Millikan tried for years to disprove this. His precise measurements of the stopping potential for sodium, lithium, and potassium confirmed Einstein's equation exactly. He gave the Nobel Committee his h measurement and his personal disbelief. Einstein won the 1921 Nobel Prize specifically for this paper.

One photon. One electron. Threshold frequency determines escape. Intensity determines count, not energy. Red lamp: sub-threshold photons, zero electrons. UV torch: above threshold, immediate emission.

card.copy:
KE = hν − φ. Below threshold: no electrons ever.
Intensity = count, not energy.
One photon acts alone.

beats.narration_text: Your turn. Take any metal — copper, gold, silver. Ask Claude to find the work function, calculate which wavelengths free electrons, and show you the threshold.

props.segment: Why a Blinding Red Lamp Releases Fewer Electrons Than a Dim UV Torch
props.command: for copper (work function 4.7 eV), calculate which visible wavelengths free electrons and which cannot, then plot KE vs frequency and mark the threshold
